join_order.description: Full sequential scan of lineitem; hash-aggregate sum(l_quantity) per l_orderkey over ~15M groups; filter sum>300 → qualifying_set of ~300-600 orderkeys
Qualifying_set is tiny (~300-600 keys). Drive all lookups from it. NO full table scans in Phase 2.
join_order.build: qualifying_set(~300-600 int32 keys, in-memory hash set)
join_order.probe: orders via orders_orderkey_hash index (point lookups only)

join_order.description: For each matching order row, look up o_custkey in customer_custkey_hash to retrieve c_name
join_order.probe: customer via customer_custkey_hash index (point lookups only)

join_order.description: For each qualifying orderkey, use lineitem_orderkey_hash to get all lineitem row positions; sum l_quantity over those positions
join_order.probe: lineitem via lineitem_orderkey_hash (multi-value index, ~4 rows per key on average)

subquery_detail.type: IN (SELECT l_orderkey FROM lineitem GROUP BY l_orderkey HAVING SUM(l_quantity) > 300)

aggregation_detail.outer_phase: flat_result_vector (~300-600 tuples, one per qualifying order)

late_materialization.phase1_subquery: Load only l_orderkey and l_quantity columns (12 bytes/row × 60M rows = 720MB); skip all other lineitem columns
late_materialization.phase2_outer: Load only o_orderdate, o_totalprice, o_custkey from orders (12 bytes/row, ~300-600 rows via index); load only c_name from customer (26 bytes, ~300-600 rows via index); load l_quantity for qualifying orderkeys via lineitem_orderkey_hash

data_structures.subquery_aggregation: compact_hash_map(pre_sized, capacity=next_power2(15000000/0.75)=33554432) — open-addressing with linear probing; key=int32_t l_orderkey, value=double sum_qty; 64 thread-local instances each pre-sized to capacity=524288 (estimated ~234K distinct keys per thread at 60% load factor)
data_structures.qualifying_set: flat_array<int32_t>(~575 entries) — after filtering sum>300; fits in L1 cache; O(1) membership via simple linear scan or small hash set
data_structures.result_tuples: std::vector<ResultTuple>(~575 entries) where ResultTuple={c_name[26], int32_t c_custkey, int32_t o_orderkey, int32_t o_orderdate, double o_totalprice, double sum_qty}

parallelism.applies_to: Phase 1 only — subquery full scan + aggregation of 60M lineitem rows
parallelism.morsel_description: 59986052 / 64 ≈ 937K rows per thread; each thread processes a contiguous chunk of the l_orderkey and l_quantity column arrays
parallelism.thread_local_accumulation: each thread maintains its own compact_hash_map<int32_t,double> for partial sum_qty aggregation
parallelism.merge_strategy: sequential_merge — after all threads complete, iterate over 64 thread-local maps and merge into single global compact_hash_map (total ~15M distinct key insertions/updates); linear cost O(sum of thread-local map sizes)
parallelism.synchronization: thread_local_merge — no shared state during scan; merge is single-threaded sequential (fast: ~15M double-add operations)
parallelism.phase2_parallelism: single_threaded — only ~300-600 qualifying orderkeys; parallelism overhead would dominate

index_usage.access_pattern: for each of ~575 qualifying orderkeys: hash probe → positions[slot.offset] = row index in orders columns → read o_orderdate, o_totalprice, o_custkey
for each of ~575 qualifying orders: probe by o_custkey → row index in customer columns → read c_name[26]
for each of ~575 qualifying orderkeys: probe by orderkey → slot.offset + slot.count → iterate positions[offset..offset+count] → sum l_quantity[pos] for each position
index_usage.cost: negligible (~575 probes × ~4 l_quantity reads each = ~2300 random reads)

phase1_scan_detail.access_pattern: strictly_sequential — two contiguous column files read front-to-back; mmap both files; 64 threads each read non-overlapping aligned ranges

memory_budget.comment_15M_entries: 15M entries × (4 bytes key + 8 bytes value + overhead) ≈ ~360MB for merged global map; thread-local maps ephemeral during parallel phase

estimated_cost.comparison_note: DuckDB=175ms, Umbra=180ms. Advantage from: (1) avoiding any full orders/customer scan — purely index-driven for Phase 2; (2) 64-thread parallel lineitem scan with morsel-driven thread-local aggregation; (3) pre-sized compact hash maps avoiding rehash; (4) mmap sequential access on l_orderkey+l_quantity only (late mat, skip other lineitem columns)
